# Abstract {.unnumbered}

International Roughness Index (IRI) is the primary metric highway agencies use to prioritize pavement
maintenance, yet nearly every machine-learning model developed to predict it has been trained on asphalt
(flexible) pavements alone, leaving rigid pavements dependent on decades-old mechanistic-empirical regression
equations. This study addresses that gap. We build a reproducible pipeline directly against the FHWA
Long-Term Pavement Performance (LTPP) Standard Data Release and assemble two matched, feature-rich panels:
1,552 flexible-pavement records across 208 sections in 55 U.S. states and Canadian provinces, and 1,693
rigid-pavement records (JPCP, JRCP, and CRCP) across 179 sections in 46 states/provinces, enriched with
Falling Weight Deflectometer joint load-transfer efficiency. An Optuna-tuned gradient-boosted ensemble is
benchmarked against a mechanistic-empirical-style regression, a random forest, and an Optuna-tuned artificial
neural network, with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) used to interpret the trained model.

Using a section-grouped train/test split that prevents repeated visits to the same physical pavement from
appearing on both sides of a fold, the ensemble explains 41.4% of out-of-sample IRI variance for flexible
pavements and 22.0% for pooled rigid pavements in a *structural* variant built from distress, structure, and
climate variables alone, rising to 69.8% and 39.1%, respectively, in a companion *operational* variant that
additionally uses each section's own recent IRI history. The two variants serve distinct engineering purposes
and are reported separately rather than conflated: the structural model isolates the effect of physical,
potentially controllable variables and is intended to support design and rehabilitation-effectiveness
evaluation, while the operational model is intended for pavement management system (PMS) use in budget
planning and inspection prioritization, where forecasting accuracy matters more than mechanism attribution.
Traffic loading and load-transfer efficiency, imputed rather than excluded, are shown by ablation to
contribute modestly but non-trivially (approximately 0.02 $R^2$) to model accuracy, and pooling JPCP/JRCP and
CRCP into a single rigid-pavement model is shown empirically to outperform fitting them separately at the
present sample size.

SHAP analysis recovers, directly from data, the same distress mechanisms that decades of mechanistic-empirical
research encoded by hand — transverse cracking and patching for flexible pavements, wheel-path faulting and
load-transfer efficiency for rigid pavements — while additionally surfacing climate interactions that
classical closed-form equations cannot represent. A series of robustness checks accompanies the headline
model: results are essentially unchanged across three gradient-boosting implementations (LightGBM, XGBoost,
CatBoost) and between single- and multiple-imputation treatment of missing data, and a controlled comparison
of naive versus group-aware train/test splitting shows that leakage-driven accuracy inflation scales with how
much genuine variance the structural features fail to explain, rather than being a fixed quantity. Two
candidate feature additions (base type, subgrade plasticity index) are tested and found not to improve on the
headline feature set. Cases in which a modeling intervention (rigid-pavement hyperparameter tuning, the
neural-network baseline, splitting JPCP/JRCP from CRCP, and the two candidate feature additions) did not
improve performance are reported with the same emphasis as those that did, consistent with the view that a
model's practical value rests on the reliability of its reported behavior, not on how favorably its results
can be selectively presented. The complete pipeline, from raw data acquisition through the trained model and
its SHAP explanations, is released as open-source code.

**Keywords:** International Roughness Index, LTPP, machine learning, SHAP, pavement engineering, flexible
pavement, rigid pavement, explainable AI

::: {.callout-tip}
## Reproducing this work
The figures and tables in this book are pre-computed by the pipeline in [`/code`](https://github.com/sabernaseralavi-60/2026_International-Roughness-Index-Prediction-Using-the-LTPP/tree/master/code)
and read as static assets in the chapters that follow, rather than re-executed at render time, so that
`quarto render` does not require a live LTPP download or a multi-minute model-training pass. Run
`python code/00_download_and_extract_all.py && python code/02_clean_and_merge.py && python code/03_eda.py
&& python code/04_model.py flexible && python code/04_model.py rigid` (plus `rigid_jpcc` / `rigid_crcp`) from
the project root to regenerate everything from scratch.
:::
